item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's… there's nothing here except the joke. This exists solely to set up a punchline. The punchline's even been done before.

This also appears to be the most popular alleges-itself-to-not-be-a-joke SCP 5001-5150. I may have forgotten one or two in there, but the community has decided this is the among the absolute best articles from what the current-day non-J SCP Wiki has to offer.

Well, okay.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This really feels like it should be a -J. No vote, but would be a +1 if it were a -J.

item.title: Re: Nitpicks
item.content:encoded:
I'm no expert at braille, but from my limited experience I would transcribe a short message like this without any caps marks (⠠⠠)

⠓⠑⠇⠏⠀⠊⠀⠉⠄⠞⠀⠋⠥⠉⠅⠬⠀⠎⠑⠑

but that works out to 41 dots.

⠠⠠⠠⠓⠑⠇⠏⠀⠊⠀⠉⠄⠞⠀⠋⠥⠉⠅⠬⠀⠎⠑⠑

works out to 44 dots, and with proper caps, though I'm not sure if the pasage caps sequence (⠠⠠⠠) is used properly here.

I imagine that it's printed out in an ASCII-art sort of style; the old BSD UNIX utility ppt(6), which prints out a picture of a punched paper tape comes to mind. Calling ASCII art UTF-8 art isn't technically wrong though, but it's kind of awkward.
There are a large number of regional and language-specific braille variants; even if you knew it was braille, analyzing text to find the right variant one sounds like a nightmare. They all do have the same 6- or 8-dot structure, though

All that being said, this does seem surprisingly realistic, if the dots are all smashed together. It's a short enough message (with close to 0 repititions) that measuring its complexity would be pretty difficult, and I don't think most people would recognized a 3x88 bitmap of braille dots as braille just by looking at it.

Anyway, great twist, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This would be a great -J article indeed, but it just doesn't belong here at all imo, sorry -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this would suit better as a Joke scp, but as someone before me said, it's always a breathe of fresh air to have an article like this on the main series +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is literally the “I can’t read I’m blind” meme

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I almost shat myself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Real life can and often is funny as hell. Crazy and hilarious things happened to people every day. The difference between a funny mainlister and a -J should be the attitude. A funny mainlister is a story that is, by its nature, funny. A -J exists pretty much solely to deliver a punchline. It's the difference between something like This Is Not Happening and a regular stand-up comedy special.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I will admit that I laughed, but as others have already said, how did the Foundation not think to try Braille?

Should definitely be a -J.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Small comment just to repeat my favorite mantra:

Mainlist SCPs can be comical; they don’t have to all be horror. There is a line between “Funny Mainlister” and “-J” and I wish someone had a succinct way to describe it.

Also, I did a reading for this one:
https://youtu.be/rngzzb82sCg

item.title: Re: Nitpicks
item.content:encoded:
Not sure how I feel about it as a whole, but I do want to say

If aliens sent us a message, I doubt they'd do it with UTF-8, a totally human construct

This is an SCP, remember?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The comedic beat in this article really fell flat for me, to be honest. While the fact that the message was Braille the whole time got a brief smile out of me when I first read this, reading back I just don't get anything else out of this article's setup. In addition, I think the message at the end felt a little awkwardly shoved into the middle , and didn't feel very naturally integrated into the rest of the article. Combined with the over-reliance of this entire article on whether the joke lands for the reader or not, the fact that it doesn't really land for me makes this a very unsubstantial article. -1.

Coming back to this one, my opinion's really flipped here, and the punchline hit for me a lot better than it previously did. I'm now a lot more comfortable upvoting this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think it's appropriate not to be a -J because despite all funny things, we still don't know where it is from after all. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Would have been great as a -J. As it is now, I'm a bit ambivalent about it. No vote because I can't have enough of light SCPs despite all.

item.title: Gottem
item.content:encoded: They had us in both halves, not gonna lie.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think this would work better as a -J, but I love how the Foundation thought of the most complicated methods first and then found out it was just braille. It's kinda meta, and I like that. The phrase "When you hear hooves, you think horses, not zebras" comes to mind.

+1 from me because I really enjoyed it, and also facepalmed when they found out it was braille.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This would of fit far much better as a -J

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Article in a nutshell.

All joking aside, there are way too many logical inconsistencies here. If the message pattern was a series of dots, how was Braille not the first thing they tried? Even then, why are the verbal and non-verbal language screeners separate? Why and how did this thing send a message in Braille? And if it knows Braille, it's probably been blind for a long time, so why does it seem distressed about it?

The Foundation was stupid and the circumstance was contrived. It was, however, extremely funny. Personally, I think this would work better as a -J.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well…I gotta say: I didn't see that comin'.

I'll be leaving - wait! I almost forgot to leave this behind: +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Perfect SCP to read on April Fools. Got a good laugh out of me. +1
Haven't laughed this hard in a while!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Take your +1 and go

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's always a breathe of fresh air reading an article like this. Funny as hell +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The situation here is impossibly contrived. As Marcsine points out, several of the analyses they did run should have put them on the right track, but inexplicably fail to provide any usable output. It doesn't work, for me, as a joke about human stupidity if the characters are arbitrarily hamstrung in this way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I had a good chuckle reading this article. The whole decoding team being dumb was great. Although I wish there was a bit more depth in what the message is actually from - mystery is good but the articles feels quite short and not very developed.
Although, I suppose it is short and sweet, and assuming you were trying to make people laugh, it does it's job. +1

item.title: Nitpicks
item.content:encoded:
I really want to like this article, but there's just a couple things that I'm not vibing with.

I don't think the 'code' itself was justified. If aliens sent us a message, I doubt they'd do it with UTF-8, a totally human construct, using Braille, also a human construct as well as one of the least common languages used with UTF-8. Even if they did know our language, this would be one of the most obtuse methods of communication short of full on encryption.

But, I'm not an alien myself, so benefit of the doubt. Here's the transmission the site would've got.

 ⠠⠠⠓⠑⠇⠏⠀⠠⠊⠀⠠⠠⠉⠄⠞⠀⠠⠠⠋⠥⠉⠅⠬⠀⠠⠠⠎⠑⠑

I'm no expert on Braille, so forgive any inaccuracies. First, this message is 50ish dots, not 44, although this could just be a case of capitalization or some contraction I'm missing. Second, if the message used Unicode as I've used here, then the translators could've just looked up the codes and found they were in Braille immediately. But then maybe it was ASCII dots. Even then, Braille is arranged in 2x3 dot blocks, not exactly a "complex position pattern".

Also, the idea that a nonverbal languages test would not be run after 248 attempts for a message explicitly in written format is a little absurd. I understand the decryption team is supposed to be a bit daft but it's ridiculous that such a suite of analyses for an alien message wouldn't include anything not uttered through vocal cords.

Also, there's a bit of an issue with the comments; some of them are explicitly written after the test, such as #2, and some explicitly written before, such as #249. It wouldn't make sense for #249's comment to be written in that way if the comment can be added or changed after the test itself.

Also also, it's sometimes unclear what "Translation Failed." even means. #3 is measuring complexity, and it just fails, implying the language is too complex to even measure. But Braille is super simple, debatably more simple than our written alphabet. #247, measuring whether the message is art, also fails, but it's totally unclear whether that means the message is art, if it isn't art, or if the system couldn't figure it out.

But you know what, still funny. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Damn it I laughed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …holy shit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This made me howl momentarily at the end. God dammit.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sometimes the answer is right in front of our fuckin noses. +1, had me laughing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i… uh… how was the foundation this stupid +1
